textPlain: The trouble with the CISC vs RISC interpretation is that the x86 was CISC but trashed all other architectures in the 1990s especially RISC architectures includinghttps://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DEC_Alphaand Intel’s ownhttps://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ItaniumI think it’s as simple as a mass market architecture that sells more units can justify more investment than anything that sells fewer units.  If DEC had been able to steal market share from the 386 with a VAX based product it might have been able to take the 386’s place but from a business perspective they didn’t want to cannabalize sales of higher margin minicomputers.  The transition from bipolar to CMOS was also difficult because it did mean a regression in performance,  IBM addressed this in the 390 by introducing a clustering solution but it was a bold and risky move.
Another link for some history from the DEC side is this [1].[1] https://simh.trailing-edge.com/dsarchive.html

If you want to learn about the history of VAX chips I very strongly recommend this oral history:Supnik, Robert oral historyhttps://www.computerhistory.org/collections/catalog/10273826...If you have any interest in VAX or DEC or chip design in the 80s. This is a must watch.Later also goes into how Alpha was created in Part 2.

x86 isn’t a VAX though, not all CISC architectures are equally complex (or RISC arch’s reduced), and VAX does have a reputation for being a particularly CISCy CISC. We don’t really know fully if the same tricks would have worked as well with it.
